items.instruction: Which circumstance is one of the listed warning-signal triggers in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 3-2-2, which condition calls for directing the general warning signal with alternating red and green lights?

choices.text: Aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists.
A pilot requests taxi instructions after landing.
A vehicle reports the movement area is clear.
A routine runway assignment is being issued.

items.explanation: Per 3-2-2a, one listed condition is that aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists.

items.instruction: Does this warning-signal statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must direct a general warning signal with alternating red and green lights to aircraft or vehicle operators, as appropriate, when mechanical trouble exists of which the pilot might not be aware.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 3-2-2b, mechanical trouble of which the pilot might not be aware is one of the listed conditions requiring the warning signal.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You observe an aircraft and a vehicle converging on the movement area in a way that creates an immediate collision hazard. Another controller says to wait and see whether the operators notice each other first.
items.para_context: Under 3-2-2, direct a general warning signal, alternating red and green, to aircraft or vehicle operators, as appropriate, when aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists or when other listed hazards are present.

choices.text: Direct the general warning signal with alternating red and green lights to the affected operator as appropriate.
Wait for the operators to sort it out on their own because the warning signal is optional.
Use a steady red signal because the paragraph requires a prohibitive signal in every hazard case.
Use no light signal unless the pilot specifically requests one.

items.explanation: Per 3-2-2, the alternating red and green warning signal is used when a collision hazard or other listed hazardous condition exists.

items.instruction: Which acknowledgment method matches the paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 3-2-3, how should a helicopter equipped with receiver only acknowledge a signal between sunset and sunrise?

choices.text: Flash the landing light or search light.
Rock the wings.
Move ailerons or rudders while on the ground.
Flash only the navigation lights.

items.explanation: Per 3-2-3b2, a helicopter between sunset and sunrise acknowledges by flashing the landing light or search light.

items.instruction: Does this receiver-only statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: For a fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only, between sunset and sunrise the controller should request the aircraft to flash navigation lights or landing lights.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 3-2-3a2, fixed-wing aircraft between sunset and sunrise acknowledge by flashing navigation or landing lights.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only is airborne during daylight hours. You need the pilot to acknowledge your light signal. Another controller suggests asking the pilot to flash navigation lights.
items.para_context: Under 3-2-3a1, a fixed-wing aircraft between sunrise and sunset acknowledges by moving ailerons or rudders while on the ground, or by rocking wings while in flight.

choices.text: Request the aircraft to rock its wings.
Request the aircraft to flash navigation or landing lights.
Request the aircraft to turn toward the controlling facility and flash a landing light.
Delay the acknowledgment request until sunset so the light signal will be easier to see.

items.explanation: Per 3-2-3a1(b), a fixed-wing aircraft in flight between sunrise and sunset acknowledges by rocking its wings.

items.instruction: Which action is specifically authorized in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 3-5-2, which action is one of the listed STOL runway procedures?

choices.text: Issue the measured STOL runway length if the pilot requests it.
Assign a designated STOL runway whenever it shortens taxi time.
Assign a STOL runway only to military aircraft.
Issue declared distances instead of measured STOL runway length on pilot request.

items.explanation: Per 3-5-2b, the measured STOL runway length is issued if the pilot requests it.

items.instruction: Does this STOL-runway statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: A designated STOL runway may be assigned only when requested by the pilot or as specified in a letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-5-2a.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A controller wants to assign a designated STOL runway to save time even though the pilot has not requested it and no letter of agreement with the operator covers that use.
items.para_context: Under 3-5-2, a designated STOL runway may be assigned only when requested by the pilot or when specified in a letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.

choices.text: Do not assign the STOL runway under those circumstances.
Assign it anyway because controller convenience is enough to use a STOL runway.
Assign it only if the runway is longer than the primary runway.
Assign it only after the aircraft lands and requests taxi instructions.

items.explanation: Per 3-5-2a, STOL runway assignment is limited to pilot request or an LOA with the operator.

items.instruction: Use Table 3-8-2 to answer this question.
items.question_text: Under Table 3-8-2, what minimum distance is required between parallel runway edges for simultaneous opposite direction operations between sunset and sunrise?

choices.text: 2,800 feet.
1,400 feet.
1,000 feet.
Not authorized.

items.explanation: Table 3-8-2 shows 2,800 feet between parallel runway edges between sunset and sunrise.

items.instruction: Does this simultaneous-operations statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers may authorize simultaneous opposite direction operations only when they are conducted in VFR conditions, two-way radio communication is maintained with the aircraft involved, and pertinent traffic information is issued.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 3-8-4a and b, VFR conditions, two-way radio communication, and pertinent traffic information are required conditions.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: After sunset, one aircraft wants to depart from a runway while another will make an opposite-direction low approach on a parallel landing strip. Another controller says the operation is fine as long as both pilots are talking to the tower.
items.para_context: Under 3-8-4 and Table 3-8-2, opposite-direction operations between sunset and sunrise are not authorized on a runway and a parallel landing strip. The operation also requires VFR conditions and two-way radio communication with pertinent traffic information.

choices.text: Do not authorize the simultaneous opposite direction operation under those circumstances.
Authorize it because two-way radio communication alone satisfies the rule after sunset.
Authorize it if the runway and strip are at least 1,400 feet apart.
Authorize it whenever one aircraft is only conducting a low approach.

items.explanation: Per Table 3-8-2, opposite-direction operations between sunset and sunrise are not authorized on a runway and a parallel landing strip.

items.instruction: Which item is one of the items forwarded under this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 3-10-2, which item is forwarded, as appropriate, to the control facility having IFR jurisdiction in your area?

choices.text: Runway in use.
Passenger count.
Fuel remaining.
Maintenance status of the aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-2a5, runway in use is one of the items forwarded as appropriate.

items.instruction: Does this citation statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 7-4-1, Visual Approach.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The visual-approach reference cited in 3-10-2 is Para 7-4-1.

items.instruction: Does this weather-forwarding statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When the weather is below 1,000 feet or 3 miles, or the highest circling minimums, whichever is greater, controllers must issue current weather to aircraft executing an instrument approach if it changes from the ATIS or from the weather previously forwarded to the center or approach control.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 3-10-2b.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Your nonapproach control facility clears an arriving IFR aircraft for a visual approach. Another controller says there is no need to pass anything to the facility holding IFR jurisdiction because the pilot has already been cleared visually.
items.para_context: Under 3-10-2a, forward the listed items, as appropriate, to the control facility having IFR jurisdiction in your area. One of the listed items is when you clear an arriving aircraft for a visual approach.

choices.text: Forward the applicable approach information to the control facility having IFR jurisdiction.
Do not forward anything because visual approach clearance ends the need for IFR coordination.
Wait until the aircraft lands, then decide whether IFR jurisdiction ever needed the information.
Forward only the runway in use and omit the visual-approach item because that paragraph is tower-only guidance.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-2a1, clearing an arriving aircraft for a visual approach is one of the items forwarded as appropriate to the IFR-jurisdiction facility.

items.instruction: Which condition is one of the listed exceptions in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 4-1-2, which condition is one of the exceptions under which altitude and distance limitations need not be applied?

choices.text: Requested routing is via an MTR.
The pilot reports the NAVAID received clearly.
The route is on an established airway.
The aircraft is at or above FL 450.

items.explanation: Per 4-1-2c, requested routing via an MTR is one of the listed exceptions.

items.instruction: Does this exception statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Altitude and distance limitations need not be applied when routing is initiated by ATC or requested by the pilot and radar monitoring is provided.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That statement appears in 4-1-2a.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A pilot requests routing that would exceed the normal altitude or distance limitations of the NAVAID being used. Radar monitoring will be provided. Another controller says the request must be denied because the limitations always apply once the route leaves an established airway.
items.para_context: Under 4-1-2a, altitude and distance limitations need not be applied when routing is initiated by ATC or requested by the pilot and radar monitoring is provided.

choices.text: The exception may be applied because the route is pilot-requested and radar monitoring will be provided.
The request must be denied because altitude and distance limitations can never be waived by radar monitoring.
The exception applies only if the pilot is GNSS-equipped and already on a random impromptu route.
The exception applies only if the route is on an MTR.

items.explanation: Per 4-1-2a, pilot-requested routing with radar monitoring is one of the listed exceptions.

items.instruction: Is this departure-terminology statement correct?
items.question_text: Avoid using the term "takeoff" except to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That instruction is the first sentence of 4-3-1.

items.instruction: Does this citation statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 3-9-9, Takeoff Clearance, and Para 3-9-11, Cancellation of Takeoff Clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the two citations listed in 4-3-1.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Before issuing the actual takeoff clearance, you need to give an aircraft a departure heading instruction. Another controller says to use the word "takeoff" in the heading instruction because the aircraft is first in line.
items.para_context: Under 4-3-1, avoid the term "takeoff" except to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance. Use terms such as "depart," "departure," or "fly" when necessary.

choices.text: Use terms such as "depart," "departure," or "fly" until the actual takeoff clearance is issued.
Use the word "takeoff" in any departure-related instruction once the aircraft is number one for departure.
Avoid any departure terminology until the aircraft starts the takeoff roll.
Use the word "takeoff" for heading and altitude instructions but not for route changes.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-1, "takeoff" is reserved for the actual takeoff clearance or canceling it; other departure instructions use terms such as "depart," "departure," or "fly."

items.instruction: Does this abnormal-approach statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: If radar contact is lost during an approach and the aircraft has not started final approach, clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That statement is the rule in 5-10-5.

items.instruction: Does this citation statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-9-2, Final Approach Course Interception.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The first reference cited in 5-10-5 is Para 5-9-2, Final Approach Course Interception.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: During a radar approach, radar contact is lost before the aircraft starts final approach. Another controller wants to continue vectors and hope the target reappears before the aircraft reaches the approach course.
items.para_context: Under 5-10-5, if radar contact is lost during an approach and the aircraft has not started final approach, clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.
Continue vectors without radar contact because the aircraft has not yet reached final approach.
Cancel the approach and instruct the aircraft to maintain VFR regardless of conditions.
Delay any action until the aircraft reports established inbound.

items.explanation: Per 5-10-5, losing radar contact before final approach requires clearing the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.

items.instruction: Which instruction is part of the succeeding timed-approach clearance in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 6-7-2a, which instruction is included when clearing the succeeding aircraft for a timed approach?

choices.text: Tell the succeeding aircraft to leave the final approach fix inbound, or the outer marker or fix used in lieu, at a specified time.
Delay release to the tower until after the aircraft crosses the final approach fix inbound.
Cancel the preceding aircraft's IFR flight plan before clearing the succeeding aircraft for approach.
Require the succeeding aircraft to remain at the holding fix until the preceding aircraft lands in every case.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-2a, the timed-approach clearance includes telling the succeeding aircraft when to leave the final approach fix inbound or the outer marker/fix used in lieu.

items.instruction: Does this citation statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-9-5, Approach Separation Responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: One of the references in 6-7-2 is Para 5-9-5, Approach Separation Responsibility.

items.instruction: Does this tower-release statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must release the aircraft to the tower before it reaches the final approach fix.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 6-7-2c.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You are sequencing timed approaches. No alternative missed approach procedure is available and the weather is less than required by 6-7-1c. The preceding aircraft is still airborne and has not canceled IFR. Another controller wants to clear the succeeding aircraft for approach now to keep the sequence moving.
items.para_context: Under 6-7-2b, if an alternative missed approach procedure is not available and weather conditions are less than required, clear the succeeding aircraft for an approach when the preceding aircraft has landed or canceled its IFR flight plan.

choices.text: Wait until the preceding aircraft lands or cancels its IFR flight plan before clearing the succeeding aircraft for approach.
Clear the succeeding aircraft now because timed approaches always continue once spacing is established.
Clear the succeeding aircraft now as long as it can maintain its assigned altitude to the holding fix.
Transfer the succeeding aircraft to the tower first, then let the tower issue the approach clearance.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-2b, without an alternative missed approach procedure and with weather below the required condition, the succeeding aircraft waits until the preceding aircraft lands or cancels IFR.

items.instruction: Which timing requirement is the one stated in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Timed-approach second aircraft requires what level-flight interval before the final approach fix?

choices.text: At least 1 minute of level flight.
At least 30 seconds of level flight.
At least 2 minutes of level flight.
No specific level-flight interval is required.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-4, the second aircraft is cleared early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before crossing the applicable fix.

items.instruction: Does this timed-approach statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must clear the second aircraft for an approach early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before the final approach fix or equivalent fix.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 6-7-4, the second aircraft is cleared early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before crossing the fix.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: You are applying 6-7-2b and the weather report indicates the second aircraft will be in IFR conditions over the final approach fix. Another controller says there is no need to worry about level-flight time as long as the aircraft is cleared before reaching the marker.
items.para_context: Under 6-7-4, clear the second aircraft early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before crossing the final approach fix, outer marker, or the fix used in lieu when the paragraph's weather criteria are met.

choices.text: Clear the second aircraft early enough to provide at least 1 minute of level flight before crossing the fix.
Clear the aircraft whenever convenient because the paragraph does not require any specific level-flight interval.
Wait until after the aircraft crosses the fix, then assign level flight for 1 minute.
Cancel the approach because timed approaches may not be used in IFR conditions over the final approach fix.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-4, the second aircraft is cleared early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before crossing the fix.

items.question_text: Under 3-2-2, which condition is one of the listed situations requiring the alternating red and green warning signal?
items.explanation: Per 3-2-2b, mechanical trouble of which the pilot might not be aware is one of the listed situations.

choices.text: Mechanical trouble exists of which the pilot might not be aware.
A runway change has been approved.
A pilot requests a light-gun acknowledgment after landing.
A vehicle has completed its runway crossing.

items.question_text: Under 3-2-2, the warning signal is not a prohibitive signal and may be followed by any other light signal as circumstances permit.
items.explanation: That statement appears in the explanatory note of 3-2-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-2-3, how should a fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only acknowledge a signal while in flight between sunrise and sunset?
items.explanation: Per 3-2-3a1(b), a fixed-wing aircraft in flight between sunrise and sunset acknowledges by rocking its wings.

choices.text: Rock wings.
Flash navigation or landing lights.
Turn toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light.
Move ailerons or rudders while on the ground.

items.question_text: Under 3-2-3, a helicopter while hovering between sunrise and sunset may acknowledge by turning toward the controlling facility and flashing the landing light or by rocking the tip path plane.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-2-3b1(a).

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Place the fixed-wing receiver-only acknowledgment methods from 3-2-3 in day/night order.
items.explanation: Per 3-2-3a, the fixed-wing acknowledgments progress from daytime ground, to daytime flight, to nighttime light signals.

choices.text: Between sunrise and sunset while on the ground: move ailerons or rudders.
Between sunrise and sunset while in flight: rock wings.
Between sunset and sunrise: flash navigation or landing lights.

items.question_text: Under 3-5-2, when may a designated STOL runway be assigned?
items.explanation: Per 3-5-2a, it may be assigned only when requested by the pilot or as specified in a letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot or as specified in a letter of agreement with an aircraft operator.
Whenever controller workload or traffic flow makes it desirable.
Only for military aircraft.
Whenever the STOL runway is longer than the primary runway.

items.question_text: Under 3-5-2, if the pilot requests the measured STOL runway length, it must be issued.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-5-2b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-8-4, between sunset and sunrise, simultaneous opposite direction operations on a runway and a parallel landing strip are:
items.explanation: Table 3-8-2 shows such operations are not authorized between sunset and sunrise.

choices.text: Not authorized.
Authorized if the runway and strip are at least 1,400 feet apart.
Authorized if both aircraft maintain two-way radio communication.
Authorized if both aircraft are only conducting low approaches.

items.question_text: Under 3-8-4, simultaneous opposite direction operations require VFR conditions and two-way radio communication with the aircraft involved, with pertinent traffic information issued.
items.explanation: That requirement comes from 3-8-4a and b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-10-2, which item is one of the items forwarded, as appropriate, to the facility having IFR jurisdiction in your area?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-2a3, cancellation of the IFR flight plan is one of the listed items.

choices.text: Cancellation of the IFR flight plan.
Aircraft gross weight.
Passenger count.
Fuel on board.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-2, when weather is below 1,000 feet or 3 miles, or the highest circling minimums, whichever is greater, current weather must be issued to aircraft executing an instrument approach if it changes from the ATIS or from previously forwarded weather information.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-10-2b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-1-2, which is one of the listed exceptions under which altitude and distance limitations need not be applied?
items.explanation: Per 4-1-2b, one listed exception is that operational necessity requires and approval has been obtained from the Frequency Management and Flight Inspection Offices to exceed them.

choices.text: Operational necessity requires and approval has been obtained from the Frequency Management and Flight Inspection Offices to exceed them.
The pilot reports the NAVAID signal is strong.
The route is on an established airway.
The aircraft is operating above FL 450.

items.question_text: Under 4-1-2, requested routing via an MTR is one of the listed exceptions.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 4-1-2c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-3-1, when may the term "takeoff" be used?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-1, use "takeoff" only to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance.

choices.text: Only to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance.
Any time an aircraft is first in line for departure.
Whenever issuing a heading or altitude to be flown after departure.
Only for route amendments, not for the takeoff clearance itself.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-1, controllers may use terms such as "depart," "departure," or "fly" in clearances when necessary.
items.explanation: That statement appears in the second sentence of 4-3-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-10-5, what should be done if radar contact is lost during an approach before the aircraft has started final approach?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-5, clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.
Continue vectors until radar contact is reestablished.
Cancel the approach and require the aircraft to remain VFR.
Transfer the aircraft to the tower and let the tower decide the next action.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-5, if radar contact is lost during an approach and the aircraft has not started final approach, clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.
items.explanation: That statement is the rule in 5-10-5.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-7-2, if an alternative missed approach procedure is not available and weather is less than required by 6-7-1c, when may the succeeding aircraft be cleared for an approach?
items.explanation: Per 6-7-2b, the succeeding aircraft is cleared when the preceding aircraft has landed or canceled its IFR flight plan.

choices.text: When the preceding aircraft has landed or canceled its IFR flight plan.
As soon as the preceding aircraft passes the final approach fix inbound.
As soon as the succeeding aircraft reaches the holding fix at the assigned altitude.
Only after the tower assumes responsibility for all aircraft in the sequence.

items.question_text: Under 6-7-2, the aircraft must be released to the tower before it reaches the final approach fix.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 6-7-2c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-7-4, how much level flight must be provided before the second aircraft crosses the final approach fix, outer marker, or the fix used in lieu when the paragraph's IFR-condition criteria are met?
items.explanation: Per 6-7-4, the required level-flight interval is at least 1 minute.

choices.text: At least 1 minute.
At least 30 seconds.
At least 2 minutes.
No specific level-flight interval is required.

items.question_text: The second aircraft must be cleared early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before the final approach fix or equivalent fix.
items.explanation: That statement is the rule in 6-7-4.

choices.text: True
False
